1: Ps 31 is one of the most liturgically influential laments in the Psalter. Its influence on NT passion narratives is extensive (v5 cited by dying Jesus; echoes in Jeremiah's confessions). The superscription 'For the director of music' (la-menatzeach) indicates professional temple performance. 'Let me never be put to shame' (al-evoshu leolam) — bosh (shame) was the public social experience of failure and loss of honor. In an honor-shame culture, public shame represented real social destruction: the shamed person lost standing, relationships, and potentially livelihood.
2: 'Turn your ear to me; quickly rescue me. Be my rock of refuge, a strong fortress' — the request for speed (mehera hatzileni) reflects the urgency of the crisis. 'Rock of refuge' (tzur maoz) and 'strong fortress' (bet metsudat) are military metaphors from siege warfare: the high rock-fortress (such as Masada, or the Judean hill fortresses) provided safety that flat terrain did not. YHWH as the ultimate fortress is a metaphor for the futility of every human military defense against divine protection.
3: 'For you are my rock and my fortress; lead me and guide me for your name's sake' — the petition 'for your name's sake' (lemaan shimecha) is a covenant argument: YHWH's name and reputation are at stake in his treatment of his servant. If YHWH abandons the one who trusts him, YHWH's character is impugned before the nations (cf. Ezekiel 36:22: YHWH acts 'for the sake of his holy name'). This rhetorical strategy — appealing to divine self-interest — is common in Psalter and prophetic intercession.
4: 'Lead me out of the net they have hidden for me, for you are my refuge' — reshet (net) — the hunting net as enemy trap. ANE hunting imagery pervades Psalter lament psalms: the psalmist as prey of hunters, the enemies as hunters who lay nets, pits, and snares. This reflects both literal practices of ancient hunting (nets were used to catch animals) and a metaphorical framework for understanding social and political persecution.
5: 'Into your hand I commit my spirit; you have redeemed me, O LORD, faithful God' — beyadechah apqid ruchi — the dying-person formula: entrusting the life-breath to divine keeping. This was likely a standard bedtime prayer or deathbed commendation formula in Second Temple Judaism. Jesus cites it as his last words in Luke 23:46, giving it its fullest weight. Paditha (you have redeemed, Qal perfect) — the redemption here is past-tense assurance, an expression of certainty of divine response typical of lament psalms. El emet (faithful God) — the God of emet (truthfulness, reliability) keeps his covenant promises.
6: 'I hate those who pay regard to worthless idols, but I trust in the LORD' — havle shav (worthless/vain idols, literally 'vapors of emptiness') — the polemic against idol-worship. The contrast between trusting in havle shav and trusting in YHWH is the fundamental religious binary of the Psalter. ANE religion offered a spectrum of patron deities one could appeal to; Psalmic theology insists on exclusive YHWH-loyalty. The word havel will become Qohelet's signature term in Ecclesiastes.
7: 'I will rejoice and be glad in your steadfast love, for you have seen my affliction; you have known the distress of my soul' — chesed (steadfast love) + divine knowing of human suffering: the combination asserts that YHWH is both loving and attentive. In ANE religion, a distant or indifferent deity was a real possibility; petitions often began with rousing the god from sleep or inattention (cf. the Baal-mockery of Elijah in 1 Kings 18:27). Ps 31 assumes YHWH's attentiveness: he has 'seen' (raita) the affliction and 'known' (yadacta) the distress — the intimate knowing of Genesis 4:1 and Exodus 3:7.
8: 'You have not delivered me into the hand of the enemy; you have set my feet in a broad place' — bammerchav (in the broad place) — the spatial metaphor of rescue as spaciousness (cf. Psalms 18:19; Psalms 118:5). ANE cities were cramped and enclosed; the open plain was associated with freedom and safety. The enemy's hand represents capture and constraint; the broad place represents delivered freedom.
9: 'Be gracious to me, O LORD, for I am in distress; my eye is wasted from grief; my soul and my body also' — the physical-psychological-spiritual triple distress: eye, soul (nephesh), body (beten, literally 'belly') are all affected. ANE lament texts regularly describe illness in terms of multiple body-parts being afflicted simultaneously: the Babylonian Ludlul Bel Nemeqi ('I will praise the Lord of Wisdom') similarly catalogs multi-member suffering.
10: 'For my life is spent with sorrow, and my years with sighing; my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away' — avoni (my iniquity) — a striking admission that the psalmist connects suffering to sin. The body-as-moral-barometer was common in ANE anthropology: illness was divine punishment for covenant violation. This is the framework that Job's friends apply (and which the book of Job then critiques). The psalmist does not debate the connection but confesses it as grounds for mercy rather than justice.
11: 'Because of all my adversaries I have become a reproach, especially to my neighbors, and an object of dread to my acquaintances' — the social dimensions of the crisis: neighbors distance themselves, acquaintances flee. In ANE society, the sick and accused were socially dangerous — associating with them risked ritual contamination or political guilt-by-association. The abandoned person becomes an object of shame (cherpah) and horror (pachad): two registers of social disaster.
12: 'I have been forgotten like one who is dead; I have become like a broken vessel' — the double death-metaphor: forgotten like the dead and shattered like a useless pot. In ANE funerary practice, broken pottery was scattered over graves and at the boundary of the underworld — the broken vessel was an image of death itself. The social memory of the community was the closest thing to 'life after death' in pre-resurrection Hebrew anthropology: to be 'forgotten like the dead' was the ultimate social erasure.
13: 'For I hear the whispering of many — terror on every side' — magor missaviv (terror on every side/all around) — this exact phrase appears in Jeremiah's confessions as a taunt applied to him (Jeremiah 20:3-4, 10): Jeremiah himself uses it sardonically and then applies it to himself. The phrase became a standard description of the experience of the persecuted prophet. The whispering (dibbat rabbim) is the slander that travels quietly through social networks, destroying reputation.
14: 'But I trust in you, O LORD; I say, You are my God' — the pivot of the psalm: the declaration of trust in the middle of the crisis. 'You are my God' is the covenant declaration — the personal appropriation of the covenant formula ('I will be your God and you will be my people'). In the midst of social abandonment and terror, the psalmist claims the covenant relationship as the counter-reality.
15: 'My times are in your hand; rescue me from the hand of my enemies and from my persecutors' — ittotai beyadechah (my times/seasons are in your hand) — a profound statement of divine providence over the calendar of the psalmist's life. The 'times' include both the difficult moments and the moments of rescue. The persecutors think they control the timing of the psalmist's fate; the psalmist claims YHWH controls it.
16: 'Make your face shine on your servant; save me in your steadfast love' — the Aaronic blessing language (Numbers 6:25-26) becomes personal petition: 'make your face shine' (haer panecha) is precisely the priestly blessing formula. The psalmist asks to receive what priests pronounced — the shining divine face as the experience of grace. Chesedechah (your steadfast love) — salvation through covenant love, not merit.
17: 'O LORD, let me not be put to shame, for I call upon you; let the wicked be put to shame; let them go silently to Sheol' — the reversal of shame: the psalmist asks that the shame intended for him be transferred to the wicked. 'Silently to Sheol' — the silence of Sheol (dumah) contrasts with the noise of the slanderers' whispering (v13). The judgment is poetic: those who used their mouths to destroy will be silenced in death.
18: 'Let the lying lips be mute, which speak insolently against the righteous with pride and contempt' — siphte shaqer (lying lips) — the legal category of false witness (ed shaqer) is implied. In the covenant court, false witnesses who deliberately harmed the innocent were to receive the punishment intended for the accused (Deuteronomy 19:16-19). The psalmist's prayer for silence of lying lips invokes this legal principle.
19: 'Oh, how abundant is your goodness, which you have stored up for those who fear you' — tzafanta (stored up) — the divine goodness is not spent but reserved and accumulated for the fearers. ANE royal storehouses (grain, oil, valuables) provided for the king's household and dependents; YHWH's goodness functions as a divine storehouse for those who are covenant-loyal. The stored goodness will be publicly displayed, vindicating the fearers before the watching community.
20: 'In the shelter of your presence you hide them from the plots of men; you store them in your shelter from the strife of tongues' — beseter panechah (in the shelter of your face/presence) and sukkah (booth/shelter) — two shelter metaphors for divine protection. The divine face (panim) as shelter creates a protective zone around the covenant-loyal. The Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) enacted this theology: the sukkah booth was a reminder of divine shelter in the wilderness. 'Strife of tongues' (riv leshonot) — legal/courtroom conflict and slander.
21: 'Blessed be the LORD, for he has wondrously shown his steadfast love to me when I was in a besieged city' — ber ir matzor (a besieged city) — the military metaphor for the crisis is now explicit. ANE siege warfare was the most terrifying military scenario: the city's food supply was cut off, disease spread, and the population faced starvation or slaughter. YHWH's chesed is 'wondrous' (hiphli = miraculous, extraordinary) precisely because it operated under conditions that seemed impossible for rescue.
22: 'I had said in my alarm, I am cut off from your sight. But you heard the voice of my pleas for mercy' — the past-tense report of the crisis-moment conviction that YHWH had severed the relationship. This is the experience of divine abandonment that v1 prays against: the psalmist believed it had happened, then discovered it had not. The gap between the felt experience of divine absence and the actuality of divine presence is a central dynamic of lament psalmody.
23: 'Love the LORD, all you his saints (chasidim)! The LORD preserves the faithful but abundantly repays the one who acts in pride' — the communal exhortation following personal rescue: the individual's experience grounds corporate instruction. 'Preserves the faithful' (notzer emunim) — emun (faithful, from aman) — the covenant-reliable person is protected by YHWH. 'Acts in pride' (gaavah, haughtiness) — the contrast with anav (humble/afflicted) throughout the Psalter: pride is the disposition of those who do not need God.
24: 'Be strong and let your heart take courage, all you who wait for the LORD' — the psalm closes with the same encouragement that closes Joshua 1 and Deut 31 ('be strong and courageous, chizqu veyimatz libbekem'). The Mosaic/Deuteronomic formula is applied to the situation of all who 'wait' (meyachalim) for YHWH — not just military warriors but all who exercise covenant patience. The psalm has moved from individual cry (v1) to communal strength-exhortation (v24): the rescue of one person becomes resource for the whole community.
